external_dependency (JARUS SORA v2.5, DO-365, CAA/MAA policy, classification model versions)
state_dependent (concurrent database writes, panel mode multi-user, consensus voting order)
findings.mitigation: Deploy test doubles for all external MOD/CAA IdP and decision-making services; use fixed-seed RNG for swarm consensus and sensor noise simulation; implement snapshot testing with configurable tolerance (±1 SAIL level for SORA, ±5% for ML confidence, ±50ms for latency); run timing-sensitive tests on dedicated isolated instances; pin external API versions (JARUS v2.5, CAP 722D v3.1, DO-365 v2.0); require 3x retry with jitter for high-risk scenarios; escalate to human ack if outcome diverges across retries
findings.reasoning: DeFOSPAM findings (125 total, 68 critical) expose foundational term ambiguity (swarm-aware, meaningful human control, containment) that cascades into test interpretation variance. Isabel's examples (EX-003, EX-006, EX-022, EX-038, EX-063, EX-067) all involve human judgment (SORA assessor, swarm consensus voting, DAA resolver confidence, ML classification detection) or tight timing windows (geofence breach <500ms, RF kill <350ms). Cerf's performance engineering (NFR-END-WB-001: 96h soak detecting memory leaks; NFR-LAT-002: 3MB/s evidence store throughput) adds endurance dimension. Snapshot testing with tolerance is the pragmatic mitigation for non-deterministic AI while maintaining safety gates.
findings.recommendation: Immediate: (1) Create test-double factory for SORA assessor, DAA resolver, swarm consensus votings, and ML classifiers with frozen decision logic. (2) Implement snapshot testing framework with configurable tolerance bands per scenario. (3) Pin all external API versions in fixture metadata. (4) Add RNG seeding to all swarm/sensor simulation scenarios. Mid-term: (5) Run high-risk scenarios twice per week to detect drift; establish baseline confidence intervals. (6) Instrument audit log write path with database metrics. (7) Integrate Cerf's 96h soak test into full suite nightly execution.

findings.finding_title: Smoke suite must validate keystone safety gates in <5 minutes; current 10-scenario design is aligned with Grace's P1 priority goals
findings.scenario: RSPEC-001, RSPEC-002, RSPEC-006, RSPEC-015, RSPEC-020, RSPEC-022, RSPEC-038, RSPEC-055, RSPEC-063, RSPEC-067
risk_factors: coverage_completeness (10 scenarios must cover 69 total; 14% sampling must hit critical paths)
execution_latency (timing-sensitive scenarios can inflate run time; need parallelization)
findings.mitigation: Allocate 10 scenarios to smoke: (1) Submission intake (RSPEC-001) — GOAL-001, (2) PackCompleteness happy (RSPEC-002) — GOAL-001, (3) Swarm-aware SORA (RSPEC-006) — GOAL-002, (4) Geofence RTL (RSPEC-015) — GOAL-003, (5) C2 consensus (RSPEC-020) — GOAL-003, (6) DAA encounter (RSPEC-022) — GOAL-002, (7) Flight termination (RSPEC-038) — GOAL-003, (8) CAA/MAA apportionment (RSPEC-055) — GOAL-006, (9) Classification handling (RSPEC-063) — GOAL-009, (10) Panel audit trail (RSPEC-067) — GOAL-015. These span all P1/P2 goals and cover critical regulatory gates (intake, SORA, safety, termination, apportionment, audit). Parallelize across 3-4 test workers to target <5min total.
findings.reasoning: Grace's goals rank GOAL-001 (intake throughput), GOAL-003 (containment), GOAL-002 (swarm safety), GOAL-006 (apportionment), GOAL-015 (panel override audit) as P1/P2. Smoke suite must be a 'canary' that catches regressions in these core workflows on every commit. 10 scenarios = 14% of 69 total; statistical coverage of 95% of critical paths is achievable with this selection. Parallelization on GitHub Actions (matrix strategy with 4 workers) can keep smoke <5min.
findings.recommendation: Implementation: (1) Allocate GitHub Actions matrix with 4 parallel workers; assign 2-3 scenarios per worker. (2) Use docker-compose spin-up <10s per test to minimize setup latency. (3) Cache dependency downloads between runs. (4) Target: smoke completes in 3-4 minutes on main branch, 5-6 minutes under load. (5) Monitor: capture p95 execution time per scenario; alert if any smoke scenario >30s (indicates new flakiness). (6) Blocking: smoke must pass before merge to staging; regression + full required before release.

findings.finding_title: GeofenceContainment specification lacks five critical design subcriteria; feature file is incomplete without design workshop
consistency_issues: Chris-language.json 'containment' definition lists 'five critical subcriteria undefined: boundary tolerance, fail-safe mechanism, design standard, environmental factors, verification approach.'
rex-refined.json geofence specs (RSPEC-020+) specify 'RTL within 500ms' but do not specify tolerance range (e.g., ±50m, ±100m?)
Isabel-examples.json EX-004 (Geofence breach) uses '50m tolerance' as example data, but this is not validated as acceptable threshold
Grace-goals.json GOAL-003 measures 'GeofenceContainment scale' but scale definition in Vibe does not address tolerance parametrization
findings.reasoning: A developer trying to implement geofence logic reads the feature file and sees 'RTL within 500ms', but cannot answer: What tolerance is acceptable? What wind speed is worst-credible? What GPS accuracy assumptions? What design standard applies (DO-254 DAL B?)? Without these clarifications, the implementation will lack regulatory defensibility. DeFOSPAM Dorothy-4 identified this as confidence 10/10 critical finding.
findings.recommendation: Schedule WS-002 (REQ005: Automatic Containment Design Specification) with: CAA airspace regulator, applicant safety engineer, autonomy engineer, test engineer. Agenda: (1) Define boundary tolerance (±X meters); (2) Specify fail-safe mechanism (independent altitude limiter?); (3) Identify design standard (DO-254 DAL B? DEFSTAN 00-970 Part 9?); (4) Map environmental factors (GPS multipath, wind prediction); (5) Define verification approach (Monte Carlo? Flight test?). Output: containment design spec, geofence tolerance matrix, verification plan.

findings.finding_title: MeaningfulHumanControl feature lacks quantified crew situational awareness and workload thresholds; specification is aspirational, not testable
consistency_issues: Chris-language.json 'meaningful-human-control' marked 'proposed' with note: 'Requires measurable criteria: situational awareness frequency, authority preservation, takeover time, workload limits.'

rex-refined.json specs for human factors (RSPEC-030+) reference 'crew authority preservation' but do not define: How often must crew see system state (10Hz? 1Hz?)? What workload threshold triggers refusal (max concurrent alarms? max decision frequency?)?
Isabel-examples.json EX-007 (override latency) shows '50ms override, 10Hz telemetry' but these numbers are not validated as acceptable minimums or maximums
Vibe 'MeaningfulHumanControl' scale definition cites 'crew authority preservation' but measurable sub-scale not provided
findings.reasoning: A CAA human-factors inspector reading the feature file for meaningful human control can see the scenario ('crew observes altitude deviation, presses button, swarm halts within 50ms'), but cannot make a go/no-go decision without knowing: Is 50ms fast enough for true control authority? Is 10Hz telemetry update frequent enough for situational awareness? DeFOSPAM Dorothy-2 identified this as confidence 10/10 critical finding. Without quantified thresholds, the specification is aspirational (good goal, not testable requirement).
findings.recommendation: Schedule WS-003 (REQ010: Meaningful Human Control & Crew Workload Definition) with: CAA human factors inspector, applicant crew/flight ops lead, test pilot, autonomy engineer, human factors specialist. Agenda: (1) Define situational awareness update frequency (Hz? seconds?); (2) Define authority preservation scope (which decisions must crew pre-approve vs approve post-hoc?); (3) Define takeover time requirement (within X seconds for which off-nominal events?); (4) Define workload limits (max concurrent decisions? max duty cycle per hour?); (5) Map failure modes where control is lost; (6) Agree on evidence collection approach. Output: Meaningful human control definition, situational awareness spec, workload assessment criteria, monitoring plan.

findings.finding_title: Geofence tolerance and C2 link-loss survivability design standard absent—automation limits undefined
findings.feature: F06 (Geofence & Containment Enforcement), F07 (C2 Link & Spectrum Assurance)
findings.value_statement: Automatic geofence enforcement and C2 link resilience are critical safety mechanisms. REQ005 and REQ006 specify 'automatic containment' and 'link loss survivable' but do not define: (1) How far beyond the geofence boundary can a swarm aircraft drift before enforcement fires? (2) How long can a swarm survive without C2 link before autonomous flight termination engages? These are design-critical parameters that must be specified before Workbench can validate applicant designs.
findings.reasoning: DeFOSPAM glossary confirms: 'containment' is marked unverified with note 'Critical safety term. Distinction between automatic containment and other forms undefined. No specification of tolerance, fail-safe assumptions, or design standards.' Performance Engineering NFR analysis confirms: C2LinkResilience and GeofenceContainment are priority-1 values with no quantitative targets (no NFR for 'containment tolerance' or 'link-loss survivability time'). Vibe Requirements benchmark notes: 'No measured data on geofence breach rates or C2 link-loss events in BVLOS swarm operations. Industry anecdata suggests 30-60 second survivability window typical, but no standard published.' Without these parameters, applicant designs may have vastly different safety margins (100m tolerance vs 1km tolerance; 10 second survivability vs 5 minute survivability) and regulator assessment will be ad-hoc and non-defensible.
findings.recommendation: Before F06 and F07 can be verified, CAA and MAA must jointly publish design standards for: (1) Geofence containment tolerance (e.g., 'breach of >50m or >5 seconds duration triggers emergency termination'); (2) C2 link-loss survivability minimum (e.g., 'autonomous flight termination must engage within 30 seconds of C2 link loss'). These should be tied to swarm-specific risk assessment (larger swarms may need tighter tolerance). Recommend CAA coordinate with EASA and JARUS to align with European standards (EASA Special Conditions, JARUS guidance). Until standards are published, Workbench should require applicants to declare geofence tolerance and C2 survivability assumptions, and flag non-compliance with pending standards.

findings.finding_title: Swarm-specific scenarios require simulation infrastructure
findings.reasoning: Monte Carlo simulation for swarm emergent behavior (10,000 trials), distributed consensus voting simulation, and C2 link loss scenarios require computationally intensive test infrastructure not typical of BDD automation. Estimated execution time 900-1200 seconds per test. Standard CI/CD pipelines will timeout or consume excessive resources.
findings.recommendation: Segregate swarm simulation scenarios into separate 'simulation' suite executed on dedicated hardware (GPU-accelerated compute nodes). Consider containerized test environment (Docker + Kubernetes) for parallel test execution. Alternative: use stubbed/mocked simulation results for fast-feedback CI; reserve full-fidelity simulation for nightly/weekly runs.

findings.finding_title: Coverage gap: RF kill-switch validation requires lab-only test environment
findings.reasoning: Independent flight termination RF kill-switch test (915 MHz transmission) cannot be executed in standard office/cloud CI/CD pipeline. Requires Faraday cage or RF-isolated lab. Cannot mock RF propagation realistically (wireless link loss is non-deterministic). Current scenarios assume 'lab-only test' but no explicit mention of test environment segregation.
findings.recommendation: Create explicit test environment classification in Gherkin: @lab-only tag for RF/hardware-dependent scenarios. Segregate from standard CI/CD (they will fail in office). Establish dedicated RF test lab with Faraday cage, RF signal generator, aircraft receiver mockup. Scenario execution: quarterly validation (not on every commit). Document lab access procedure and safety protocols (RF exposure limits per FCC). Backup strategy: partner with aircraft manufacturer for HIL test bench access.

findings.finding_title: Regression suite covers 55 scenarios (80% of 69); must execute <30 minutes and block main-branch merge
findings.scenario: RSPEC-001 through RSPEC-055 (all major-severity, AC1-AC8 paths, geofence/C2/termination failures, panel overrides, apportionment, classification)
risk_factors: execution_latency (55 scenarios at 30s each = 27 minutes; parallelization required)
database_contention (panel database, audit log, classification tier transitions can lock under concurrent load)
findings.mitigation: Allocate 8-10 parallel workers; assign 5-7 scenarios per worker. Isolate test databases per worker (no shared pool). Use transaction rollback after each scenario to prevent state leakage. Implement retry logic: 2x auto-retry on database lock, human ack required on persistent failure before merge. Monitor: track test-database lock wait time per scenario; alert if any scenario >60s (indicates contention). Target: regression completes in 25-30 minutes.
findings.reasoning: Regression suite bridges smoke and full: includes all major-severity scenarios (69 all tagged @major @regression), all AC1-AC8 pass/fail paths (Isabel's examples demonstrate twins: Scotland approval vs SORA refusal end-to-end), geofence/C2/termination failure modes (GOAL-003 containment assurance), panel override audit trails (GOAL-015 panel decision governance), apportionment (GOAL-006), classification handling (GOAL-009). Must run before merge to main to ensure release readiness. 55 scenarios = 80% coverage; remaining 14 scenarios (RSPEC-056-069) are edge cases, low-priority features, and full NFR tests (reserved for nightly full suite).
findings.recommendation: Implementation: (1) Allocate 8-10 parallel GitHub Actions jobs. (2) Each job gets dedicated test PostgreSQL instance (docker-compose) with 10-20s spin-up. (3) Divide 55 scenarios among jobs; use scenario sharding by ID range (RSPEC-001-006, RSPEC-007-012, ..., RSPEC-049-055). (4) Implement database transaction rollback after each scenario to avoid state bleed. (5) Capture per-scenario execution time histogram; flag any scenario >45s (target p95 <30s). (6) Required check: regression suite must pass before main merge (blocking). (7) Required check: Allure dashboard >95% pass rate by feature before release. (8) Required check: Performance Gate 3 (1h load baseline) must pass in last main-branch test run.

findings.finding_title: Twin-sample regression (Scotland approval vs SORA refusal) must be required check on every PR to validate end-to-end regulatory paths
findings.scenario: RSPEC-002 (PackCompleteness happy: Scotland VLOS approval) paired with RSPEC-003 (PackCompleteness error: BVLOS Swarm Swarm-Aware SORA mismatch → refusal)
risk_factors: end_to_end_validation (twin tests must exercise same code paths but with different inputs; divergence indicates logic error)
regulatory_defensibility (twins demonstrate that system correctly distinguishes approval vs refusal; critical for CAA audit)
findings.mitigation: Add required check to GitHub Actions workflow on every PR: 'twin-sample regression (Scotland → SORA)'. Both twins must pass (Scotland case must result in INTAKE_COMPLETE; BVLOS Swarm case must result in RETURN_FOR_CLARIFICATION). Capture decision records and audit trails for both; compare decision reasoning to ensure divergence is justified by evidence difference, not code bug. Alert on outcome mismatch or reasoning inconsistency.
findings.reasoning: Isabel's examples (EX-001: Scotland VLOS pack complete, → INTAKE_COMPLETE; EX-002: BVLOS Swarm pack incomplete swarm-specific sections, → RETURN_FOR_CLARIFICATION) form a regulatory twin pair. Grace's GOAL-001 (Build Defensible Approval Throughput) and GOAL-002 (Assure Swarm-Aware Safety) both hinge on correct distinction between approval and refusal pathways. DeFOSPAM critical findings (swarm-aware undefined, meaningful human control undefined, containment undefined) mean the *difference* between Scotland (simple single-aircraft VLOS) and BVLOS Swarm (complex multi-agent) is a key validation point. Twin tests ensure that when evidence quality or swarm-awareness assessment changes, the system still correctly routes cases to the right outcome.
findings.recommendation: Implementation: (1) Add GitHub Actions workflow step after smoke suite passes: run twin-sample regression. (2) Both twins run in parallel (30s each). (3) Assertions: Scotland case outcome = INTAKE_COMPLETE + decision_record exists + audit trail complete. BVLOS case outcome = RETURN_FOR_CLARIFICATION + coaching feedback sent + evidence gap list populated. (4) Comparison: extract decision reasoning from both cases; ensure divergence is evidence-driven (Scotland has all 8 artefacts; BVLOS missing swarm-specific sections), not code-driven. (5) Fail PR if outcomes diverge unexpectedly (e.g., Scotland → RETURN_FOR_CLARIFICATION indicates regression in artefact detection). (6) Escalate mismatched reasoning to human code review.

findings.finding_title: MLForensicExplainability specification assumes perfect explainability is possible; no fallback strategy for deep learning
findings.feature: F10: ML/AI Assurance Case & Model Manifest
consistency_issues: Chris-language.json 'forensically-explainable' marked 'proposed' with note: 'Perfect post-hoc explainability for deep learning is infeasible. Requires workshop to define acceptable techniques and fallback.'

rex-refined.json ML assurance scenarios (RSPEC-040+) require 'forensic explainability' but do not define: Which explainability techniques are acceptable (LIME? SHAP? Saliency maps)? What if model is inherently opaque (black-box deep network)?
Isabel-examples.json lacks any example of ML explainability failure or fallback scenario
Vibe 'MLForensicExplainability' scale definition assumes explainability is achievable; no contingency for models that cannot be fully explained
findings.reasoning: A developer implementing an ML safety case reads the feature file and sees 'frozen model manifest with explainability evidence', but cannot answer: Is LIME sufficient? If SHAP analysis shows non-linear decision boundaries, is that 'explained' enough? What if the model is a deep neural network where no post-hoc explanation technique provides high confidence? DeFOSPAM Dorothy-6 identified this as confidence 10/10 critical finding. Without a fallback strategy (e.g., 'if explainability confidence < 80%, then model cannot be used for safety-critical decisions'), the specification creates an impossible requirement.
findings.recommendation: Schedule WS-006 (REQ009: ML Model Assurance & Explainability) with: CAA AI/ML assurance specialist, applicant ML engineer, test engineer. Agenda: (1) Identify all ML models in swarm architecture (vision, coordination, RTL decision); (2) For each model, define training data, validation data, test data; (3) Define forensic explainability approach (LIME? SHAP? Saliency maps? Bayesian analysis?); (4) Identify models that cannot be fully explained; (5) Define fallback approach for opaque models (restrict to non-safety-critical decisions? require smaller SAIL level?); (6) Define frozen model manifest (version, training data hash, validation results, known limitations); (7) Define model manifest retention and security. Output: AMLAS case structure, frozen model manifest specification, explainability approach with fallback strategy, SACE evidence mapping.

findings.finding_title: ML Model Manifest ('frozen model') requirements not standardized; applicants do not know what data, architecture, training scope, and explainability evidence to provide
findings.feature: F10: ML/AI Assurance Case & Model Manifest
findings.reasoning: REQ009 requires 'forensically explainable' ML models with 'frozen model manifest', but manifest structure is undefined. Chris language defines 'frozen model' but notes 7 subcriteria undefined: data retention, duration, security, manifest format, proprietary handling, ownership, reconstruction timing. Example EX-002 (Spadeadam case) is missing ML Model Manifest entirely, causing pack to fail completeness check. Example EX-014 (hypothetical refusal) applicant claims 95% explainability via LIME/SHAP but provides no evidence of off-distribution robustness. Applicants have no template or exemplar of what a 'sufficient' ML manifest looks like. AMLAS standard is referenced in REQ009 but not published yet (as of 2026-Q2).
findings.recommendation: Publish CAP 722D Addendum 3 (ML Model Manifest Template) specifying: (1) mandatory sections: model architecture (layers, weights count, hyperparameters), training data scope (size, domain, augmentation), testing scope (validation set, test set, out-of-distribution testing), explainability approach (decision-tree rules vs feature-importance rankings vs saliency maps per model type), version control (git commit hash of frozen weights). (2) Format template (3-page DOCX with fillable sections). (3) Explainability acceptance criteria: decision-trees = 100% path coverage; neural networks = >= 80% decision-path coverage via LIME/SHAP; SVMs = feature importance + decision boundary visualization. (4) Case study: publish example ML manifest for a simple swarm de-confliction model (15 pages, showing frozen weights, training data schema, LIME explainability evidence). Publish by 2026-Q2. In interim, require applicants to submit draft manifest to CAA by 2026-Q2 for feedback.

findings.finding_title: Shared step opportunities reduce automation maintenance burden
findings.reasoning: Analysis of all 17 feature files identified 12 recurring step patterns (e.g., 'Given clause library version is pinned to 2.1', 'When case status transitions to...', 'Then audit log records'). These steps appear 3-15 times across features. Factoring into shared library will improve maintainability and reduce duplication.
findings.recommendation: Create shared step library in Behave conftest.py with 12 reusable step definitions. Document parameter syntax for case status, timeline, audit log events. Estimated effort: 20 hours shared library development; 30% reduction in future step maintenance.

findings.finding_title: UK sovereign infrastructure constraints affect framework selection
findings.reasoning: Specification implies UK sovereign cloud (Google Cloud Storage with geo-redundancy, CAA Secure Archive). Standard open-source Gherkin frameworks (Cucumber, Behave, SpecFlow) assume public cloud (AWS, Azure) or on-prem deployment. GCS integration requires custom bindings. MOD classified trials require air-gap or classified cloud (e.g., UK IL5 equivalent). Dual-framework strategy (Behave for unclassified, isolated instance for classified) increases complexity.
findings.recommendation: Recommendation 1: Adopt Behave (Python) as primary framework. Justification: Python has strong GCS SDK (google-cloud-storage); classification-aware feature file plugins feasible. Recommendation 2: Create abstraction layer for artifact storage (interface abstraction, swappable GCS/local-storage backend) to support air-gapped classified trials. Recommendation 3: Plan separate test instances (public Workbench + classified Workbench) with shared step library but separate execution contexts.

findings.finding_title: Phase gate structure undefined—no specification of evidence requirements, trigger criteria, or reversibility mechanics
findings.feature: F16 (Crew Training & Competency), F26 (Panel Override & Decision Logging), F29 (Withdrawal & Re-opening Capability)
findings.value_statement: Phased trial gates are intended to enable progressive evidence accumulation and reduce approval cycle time (Vibe target: 60 days from submission to authorization). However, REQ016 specifies 'phased gates' without defining: (1) What are the phases? (2) What evidence must be complete before advancing? (3) What justifies skipping a phase? (4) Can previously-approved phases be re-opened if new risks emerge? (5) How far back can re-opening go? Without specification, implementers will guess at phase structure, and each trial will follow a different pathway.
findings.reasoning: DeFOSPAM analysis shows REQ016 source stories do not specify phase structure, trigger criteria, or reversibility rules. Vibe Requirements define PhasedGateReversibility as a critical value but note: 'Phase gate structure not specified; unclear what evidence triggers advancement. Override criteria not defined. Re-opening reversibility mechanics not specified.' Performance Engineering NFR analysis confirms: no quantitative targets for gate progression cycle time or re-opening frequency. This is implicit scope that will expand during implementation.
findings.recommendation: Before F16, F26, F29 can be implemented, CAA must publish Phase Gate Standard specifying: (1) Five phases: (Phase 0) Intake & Completeness Check; (Phase 1) Technical Review (SORA, Safety Case, Crew Training complete); (Phase 2) Operational Readiness (Containment validation, C2 link testing); (Phase 3) Operational Approval (Trial authorization issued); (Phase 4) Full Deployment. (2) Per-phase evidence gates: Phase 0 → all mandatory documents present; Phase 1 → SORA setpoints approved, Safety Case gaps resolved; Phase 2 → containment and C2 link tested with witness observation; Phase 3 → crew sign-off and insurance issued; Phase 4 → full operation with ongoing reporting. (3) Override authority: Director can skip Phase 2 if Risk Assessment score < low; DCS can override decision at Phase 3 with CAAir approval. (4) Re-opening rules: evidence can be invalidated if Risk Assessment updates materially (e.g., new hazard discovered); re-opening resets to earliest affected phase; maximum 2 re-openings per case before resubmission required. (5) Clause library versioning: cases locked to regulation version at intake; new regulation version applies only to new cases or via explicit upgrade (operator opts-in).

findings.finding_title: ML assurance case scope assumes AMLAS/SACE applicability to swarm autonomy—standards coverage unclear
findings.value_statement: REQ009 specifies AMLAS/SACE (Autonomous Machine Learning Assurance Standard / Safety and Certification Evidence) case for ML components. However, AMLAS/SACE are designed for individual-agent ML (e.g., single autonomous vehicle perception). Swarm autonomy involves emergent collective behavior (formation control, loss-of-separation avoidance, cooperative decision-making) that AMLAS/SACE may not fully cover. Applicants and regulators lack clarity on: (1) What swarm-specific ML assurance evidence is required beyond AMLAS/SACE? (2) How do collective behavior and emergent failures factor into ML assurance? (3) Are there separate assurance cases for individual aircraft vs. swarm coordination algorithms?
findings.reasoning: DeFOSPAM finding: 'Assurable and assure not operationalized with success criteria (confidence 9, critical).' REQ009 user story states 'ML components are assurable and forensically explainable' but does not define what 'assurable' means for swarm ML. Vibe Requirements note: 'AMLAS/SACE case mandatory but guidance not yet published; assumption that these standards apply to swarm autonomy.' Performance Engineering analysis confirms: MLForensicExplainability value defined but NFR targets depend on knowing what AMLAS/SACE requires for swarms. Industry anecdata: AMLAS v1.0 (2021) and SACE (draft) do not explicitly address swarm emergent behavior; EASA SC on autonomous swarms under development.
findings.recommendation: Before F10 can be verified, CAA must publish Swarm ML Assurance Guidance clarifying: (1) AMLAS/SACE case scope for individual aircraft ML (perception, mode selection); (2) Separate assurance case for swarm coordination algorithms (formation control, de-confliction, collective decision-making); (3) Evidence of emergent-behavior testing (simulation, flight test); (4) Forensic explainability requirements for collective decisions (audit trail of which aircraft influenced which decision); (5) Interaction with GOAL-004 (human control): what ML decisions require human override vs. autonomous authority. Recommend CAA coordinate with EASA on SC and JARUS WG3 on swarm assurance guidance. Until guidance is published, Workbench should include a 'Swarm ML Assessment Placeholder' that accepts applicant AMLAS/SACE cases and flags swarm-specific gaps (no emergent-behavior evidence, no swarm coordination assurance case, no forensic audit trail of collective decisions).

findings.finding_title: Completeness definition scope creep: 'complete pack' ambiguous for swarm operations—document requirements not yet standardized
findings.value_statement: F01 and F02 require pack completeness assessment against mandatory document list. For PDRA (Proposed Drone Regulations 2016) operations, document list is stable (ConOps, SORA, Safety Case, Crew Training). For BVLOS Swarm operations, document requirements are evolving: applicants must provide additional evidence (Swarm Decision-Making Architecture, Emergent-Behavior Manifest, Multi-Agent Loss-of-Separation Analysis) that industry has not yet standardized. Vibe target is 90% first-submission completeness by Q3 2026, but 'complete' is undefined for swarms.
findings.reasoning: DeFOSPAM glossary: 'Operational Authorisation pack' definition includes 'complete' but notes 'Complete used in two contexts with different implied meanings (CRITICAL).' Dorothy-8 finding: 'Complete used in two contexts with different implied meanings (REQ001: application completeness vs REQ012: data logging completeness).' Vibe Requirements confirm: PackCompleteness scale defines 'complete' as 'all mandatory document types and sections present', but mandatory document list is operation-type-dependent and list for BVLOS Swarm not yet published. Industry anecdata (AMLAS, SACE pilots): swarm packs typically rejected on first submission due to missing swarm-specific evidence (~30-50% first-submission acceptance for swarms vs. 70-80% for PDRA).
findings.recommendation: Before F02 can be verified, CAA must publish mandatory document checklist per operation type: (1) PDRA-01 VLOS: ConOps (5 pages), SORA (3 pages), Safety Case (10 pages), Crew Training Plan, Personnel Credentials, Insurance. (2) BVLOS Swarm SAIL-V: same as PDRA-01 PLUS Swarm Decision-Making Architecture (swarm coordination algorithm, agent interaction rules), Emergent-Behavior Manifest (simulation results showing behavior bounds under nominal and off-nominal scenarios), Loss-of-Separation Cascade Analysis (multi-agent hazard tree), ML Assurance Case (AMLAS/SACE plus swarm-specific evidence). (3) BVLOS beyond-SAIL-V: same as SAIL-V PLUS MOD classification handling plan (REQ018), Environmental Assessment (REQ019). Vibe target: 90% first-submission completeness presumes this checklist is published 6 months before Workbench goes live (by 2026-06-30). Until then, Workbench should include 'Swarm Document Requirements Placeholder' that warns applicants of pending document checklist and offers pre-submission template check.

findings.finding_title: Geofence tolerance and containment action latency not specified; examples show 50m tolerance and 500ms deadline, but are these enforced across all trials?
findings.reasoning: REQ005 requires 'fail-safe containment' and 'geofence enforcement', but tolerance (how far beyond geofence before action triggers?) and latency (how fast must action execute?) are not defined. Vibe GeofenceContainment scale targets '99.9% success rate' but does not specify tolerance or latency budget. Example EX-004 (Spadeadam trial) assumes 50m tolerance and 500ms latency, but is this binding for all trials, or is it trial-specific? If Scotland PDRA-01 trial (EX-001) operates at 500m VLOS distance with 100m geofence tolerance, does it use the same budget? If MOD future trials (EX-009) use 200m tolerance and 1000ms latency, is that permitted? Applicants have no specification and are setting their own tolerances.
findings.recommendation: Develop CAP 722D Section 6.3 (Geofence Containment Specification) defining: (1) tolerance bands by operation type [PDRA-01 VLOS: 10m tolerance max; BVLOS single-aircraft: 25m; BVLOS swarm: 50m]. (2) Latency budget by airspace type [populated area: 300ms; sparsely populated: 500ms; uninhabited: 800ms]. (3) Fail-safe action logic (RTL vs descend vs hold depends on altitude, wind, swarm state). (4) Verification approach (Monte Carlo simulation of 1,000 scenarios per operation type, showing >= 99.9% of scenarios result in zero boundary exceedance). Publish CAP 722D Addendum 2 by 2026-Q3. Update Workbench Artefact Detector to flag ConOps if geofence tolerance is outside spec range for operation type.

findings.finding_title: Phased trial gates and reversibility conditions not specified; applicants cannot plan evidence submission strategy across Phase 1 → Phase 2 → Phase 3
findings.feature: F18: Phased Trial Gates & Progressive Evidence Build
findings.reasoning: REQ016 requires 'phased trial gates' and 'reversibility', and Vibe PhasedGateReversibility scale targets '100% of trials proceed through documented phase gates'. But gate definitions are absent from REQ. Example EX-013 shows Phase 1 (VLOS 2-aircraft) → Phase 2 (BVLOS 5-aircraft), with conditions (crew training, CAA observer, C2 continuity) and review triggers (10 successful flights, incident-triggered re-assessment, workload limit breach). But is this gate structure binding for all trials, or is it trial-specific? If applicant wants to skip Phase 1 and go straight to Phase 2 (e.g., applicant has prior experience), is that permitted? What evidence is required at each phase gate? Applicants have no specification and no exemplars.
findings.recommendation: Develop CAP 722D Section 7 (Phased Trial Structure) specifying: (1) default gate structure for PDRA-01 (1 phase, approval terminal), BVLOS single-aircraft (Phase 1 VLOS validation → Phase 2 BVLOS), BVLOS swarm (Phase 1 VLOS 2-aircraft → Phase 2 BVLOS 5-aircraft → Phase 3 full autonomy). (2) Evidence requirements per phase (Phase 1: crew training, manual control, ConOps; Phase 2: autonomous contingency, C2 resilience, DAA performance; Phase 3: emergent behavior bounds, ML explainability, swarm consensus). (3) Gate conditions (mandatory, operation-type-specific) and optional conditions (imposed by panel, e.g., 'CAA observer required'). (4) Reversibility rules: if Phase 1 incident occurs, phase rolls back to investigation; if Phase 2 emergent behavior exceeds bounds by >20%, gate to Phase 3 denied. (5) Publish 5 exemplar gate structures (VLOS, single-BVLOS, BVLOS-swarm, MOD-classified, commercial). Publish by 2026-Q3. In interim, apply Spadeadam Phase 1/2 structure (EX-013) as default for new BVLOS swarm trials.

findings.finding_title: Classification handling (REQ018) lacks user story, process definition, and roles; applicants do not know how MOD-classified trials are reviewed or segregated
findings.reasoning: REQ018 states 'MOD classification handling per CAP 722H and JSP 440' but provides no detail. Grace's goals include GOAL-009 (manage classified MOD evidence) but no acceptance criteria. Chris language does not define classification handling terms. Example EX-010 (hypothetical MOD trial with SECRET sensor specs) shows classification detection, CRO review, and evidence segregation, but these are inferred from CAP 722H/JSP 440, not explicit in requirements. Applicants with classified evidence have no guidance on: (1) how to mark submissions, (2) who reviews classified docs (cleared staff only?), (3) how evidence is segregated (physical security? file encryption?), (4) what gets redacted for public decision records. MAA and MOD programs need clarity on how classified trials interface with standard CAA approval process.
findings.recommendation: Develop CAP 722D Addendum 4 (Classification-Aware Trial Review Process) specifying: (1) submission marking: applicants must mark envelopes and documents per CAP 722H Table H-1 (OFFICIAL-SENSITIVE, SECRET, TOP-SECRET). (2) intake process: CAA Classification Review Officer (CRO) reviews classification marking during intake; if document is classified higher than envelope marking, CMS segregates it into secure-access pool. (3) reviewer access control: only cleared CAA/MAA personnel (e.g., security-cleared leads) can view classified documents. (4) evidence segregation: create two case-review pathways (unclassified OFFICIAL-SENSITIVE for all case officers, classified SECRET for cleared subset). (5) decision record redaction: decision record is unclassified summary (redacted); classified findings documented in secure-only internal log. (6) roles: define Classification Review Officer role (cleared to SECRET, CAA/MAA assigned), classified-document handler (cleared, receipt/storage responsibility). Publish by 2026-Q3. In interim, flag any future trial submissions with MOD classification as requiring CRO review (manual step).

findings.finding_title: RSPEC-008: Vague language reduces testability
findings.reasoning: Then clause uses conditional language (should, may, might) instead of deterministic assertions, making pass/fail criteria ambiguous.
findings.recommendation: Replace with definite statements: "system should record timestamp" → "the system records timestamp and actor"

findings.finding_title: Panel deliberation scenarios resist full automation
findings.reasoning: Panel deliberation requires human judgment, discussion, and subjective override decisions (e.g., 'John Smith overrides AI flag because he found ConOps evidence'). Cannot be fully automated; too much domain expertise and contextual reasoning required. Current scenarios assume deterministic outcomes (APPROVE_WITH_CONDITIONS, REFUSE), but real panel decisions involve committee votes, dissent, escalation negotiations.
findings.recommendation: Adopt hybrid automation model for panel features: (1) Automate setup/teardown (case briefing, evidence assembly). (2) Manual testing for deliberation path (panel meets in-person, records decision via form/template). (3) Automate decision record validation (database assertions on decision object, audit log integrity). (4) Consider optional 'recorded deliberation replay' test mode where historical panel decisions are replayed to verify outcome consistency. Estimate: 40% automation coverage (setup + validation), 60% manual (deliberation logic).

findings.finding_title: GDPR compliance testing absent from feature suite
findings.reasoning: Feature F19 mentions 'DPIA' and 'GDPR compliance' in scenario text, but no Gherkin scenarios explicitly test GDPR requirements (right to access, erasure, portability, consent withdrawal). Feature is descriptive but not testable. GDPR compliance is high-stakes regulatory requirement; gaps could expose CAA to enforcement action.
findings.recommendation: Add dedicated feature file 'features/governance/gdpr_compliance.feature' with scenarios for: (1) Right to Access (user requests flight data, system returns within 30 days). (2) Right to Erasure (user requests deletion post-trial, system confirms removal from archives except legal holds). (3) Consent Management (community member withdraws feedback consent, system redacts from lessons-learned). (4) Data Minimization (system enforces retention schedule: flight data purged after 5 years unless incident-related). Each scenario should assert both positive (requirement met) and negative (breach detected/logged).

findings.finding_title: Cypress vs Behave split increases framework maintenance
findings.reasoning: Current automation design calls for mixed Cypress (front-end UI) + Behave (back-end service + API). This split requires maintaining two runtime environments, two step definition libraries, and cross-framework data-sharing. Team expertise must span TypeScript/JavaScript (Cypress) and Python (Behave). Integration testing between UI and API becomes non-trivial (Cypress cannot natively interact with Behave test data fixtures).
findings.recommendation: Consolidation strategy: Adopt Behave + Selenium (Python only) instead of Cypress. Justification: Single language (Python), shared test data fixtures, unified CI/CD pipeline. Trade-off: Playwright (TypeScript) offers superior performance vs Selenium; but cost of maintaining TypeScript + Python outweighs speed benefit. Alternative: if UI performance critical, use Behave + Playwright via Python bindings (playwright-python library). Phased approach: Phase 1 (Behave for API/service layers), Phase 2 (add Selenium for UI), Phase 3 (optional Playwright migration if UI performance becomes bottleneck).

findings.finding_title: Full suite (69 scenarios + NFR gates) requires nightly execution and integration with Cerf's 8 performance gates
findings.scenario: All 69 RSPEC scenarios + Cerf performance gates (Gate 1-8) + soak/stress/spike tests
findings.estimated_duration: 6000-7200 seconds (1.5-2 hours with parallelization and endurance tests)
risk_factors: execution_latency (96h soak test, stress test 2x peak load, spike test 3x peak)
resource_contention (96h soak requires isolated infrastructure; spike test competes with other tests)
findings.mitigation: Schedule full suite nightly 0200 UTC (off-peak); allow 2-hour execution window. Parallelize functional tests (55 regression + 14 edge cases) on 8 workers; run performance gates sequentially after functional tests complete. Allocate dedicated staging environment for NFR tests (soak, stress, spike); no resource sharing with smoke/regression. Implement alerts: email + Slack if any gate fails or flake rate >2%. No auto-retry for full suite; manual investigation required.
findings.reasoning: Full suite captures: (1) all 69 refined scenarios (functional coverage), (2) all K1-K8 keystones (safety assurance), (3) all AC1-AC8 acceptance criteria, (4) Isabel's 15 examples end-to-end, (5) Cerf's 8 performance gates (NFR-LAT-001: setup latency <3s, NFR-CAP-WB-001: load baseline p95 <3.5s, NFR-END-WB-001: 96h soak <5% drift, etc.), (6) classification boundary scenarios across all 3 tiers (OFFICIAL, OFFICIAL-SENSITIVE, SECRET), (7) 24h endurance extract for audit log forensics. Nightly execution detects issues early; mandatory before release.
findings.recommendation: Implementation: (1) Create nightly trigger in GitHub Actions: cron 0 2 * * * (0200 UTC). (2) Allocate 8 parallel workers for functional tests; each gets dedicated test DB + mocked external APIs. (3) Dedicate separate staging environment for Cerf NFR gates (soak/stress/spike); resource isolation critical for validity. (4) Sequence: functional tests (55 min) → performance gates (60 min) → alerts (if any failure). (5) Capture JUnit XML, Allure report, perf-timeline.json, flake-register.json. (6) Dashboard: display pass rate by feature, avg exec time, flake rate, coverage %, clause-lib version, override rate, perf gate status. (7) Alert thresholds: flake rate >2%, any perf gate red, clause-lib version mismatch. (8) Required check for release: full suite passed in last 7 days + all gates green + flake rate <2%.

findings.finding_title: Performance gates (Cerf Phase 4-8) must be automated parallel checks in full suite; audit log write latency <500ms is critical for GOAL-015
findings.scenario: RSPEC-067 (Panel-action audit log write latency <500ms) + Gates 1-8 from openperformance-results.json
findings.estimated_duration: 1200-1800 seconds (integrated with functional tests; parallel execution)
risk_factors: latency_criticality (audit log <500ms is SLA; panel override decisions must be immutable + fast)
gate_interdependencies (Gate 4 stress test 2x peak; Gate 5 96h soak; both can mask each other if one fails)
findings.mitigation: Integrate all 8 gates as parallel checks in full suite: Gate 1 (unit perf <100ms), Gate 2 (workspace creation <3s), Gate 3 (1h load baseline p95 <3.5s), Gate 4 (stress 2x peak graceful degradation), Gate 5 (96h soak <5% drift), Gate 6 (audit log p95 <500ms), Gate 7 (coverage matrix inference <5s), Gate 8 (evidence store PUT >3 MB/s). Each gate has its own alert threshold. Capture latency histogram (p50, p95, p99) per gate. Fail release if any gate red.
findings.reasoning: Cerf's performance engineering (openperformance-results.json: NFR-END-WB-001 96h soak, NFR-LAT-001 setup <3s, NFR-CAP-WB-001 load baseline, NFR-LAT-002 3 MB/s throughput) directly maps to operational SLAs. Isabel's EX-011 (panel override audit trail <500ms write latency) is GOAL-015 requirement. Noyce's NFR-LAT-001 and NFR-LAT-002 are capacity budgets. Ada's NFR-CAP-WB-001 is load baseline. These gates are not optional; they define system readiness. Integrating gates into CI ensures every release is validated against performance SLAs.
findings.recommendation: Implementation: (1) Add GitHub Actions job 'Performance Gates' after functional tests pass. (2) Run gates in parallel (or sequence if resource-constrained): Gate 1 (unit tests, <1min), Gate 2 (workspace creation load test, <5min), Gate 3 (1h load baseline, <60min), Gate 4 (stress test 2x peak, <10min), Gate 5 (96h soak, <5h in pre-prod), Gate 6 (audit log latency benchmark, <5min), Gate 7 (coverage matrix batch inference, <10min), Gate 8 (evidence store PUT throughput, <10min). (3) Allocate dedicated staging environment for soak test (Gate 5). (4) Capture latency histograms (p50, p95, p99) per gate; store as artifact. (5) Alert threshold: any gate red or p95 >threshold. (6) Required for release: all gates green in last pre-release full run; no red gates.

findings.finding_title: Clause-library version pinning critical: must pin version per scenario to track regulatory evolution; add to release notes
findings.scenario: All 69 scenarios + decision records affected by clause-library changes
risk_factors: version_coupling (clause-library v2.0 → v2.1 may change REQ definitions; old cases must be re-validated if version changes mid-trial)
audit_traceability (GOAL-012: auditors must know which clause version justified which approval decision)
findings.mitigation: Add clause-library version pin to every test run fixture and every decision record. Version pin format: 'clause-lib:v2.1' in scenario metadata. At runtime, assert that fixture version matches database version; alert if mismatch detected. Capture version pin in Allure report dashboard. In release notes, document: 'This release validates all 69 scenarios against clause-library v2.1; backward compatibility maintained for in-flight cases on v2.0'. Require explicit action to upgrade in-flight cases to new clause version.
findings.reasoning: Grace's GOAL-007 (Phased Trial Build-Up) and GOAL-012 (Ensure Evidence Traceability) both require version tracking. Isabel's EX-015 (post-decision archive) specifies 'clause-library version pin, extract deliberation log' as archival requirements. GOAL-005 (Incident Investigation) requires AAIB to know which regulatory version justified each decision. DeFOSPAM critical findings (swarm-aware undefined, meaningful human control undefined) suggest regulatory definitions will evolve; version pinning ensures historical decisions remain defensible even after definitions change.
findings.recommendation: Implementation: (1) Add version metadata to every scenario fixture: {clause_lib_version: 'v2.1', pinned_at_runtime: '2026-04-23T02:15:00Z'}. (2) At test runtime, assert fixture version matches config.clause_lib_version; fail if mismatch. (3) In Allure dashboard, add panel 'Clause Library Version Pin': display version string per test run; alert if any run uses different version than previous. (4) In release notes, document clause-lib version used for regression test baseline. (5) For future upgrades (v2.1 → v2.2): create migration plan for in-flight cases; require explicit re-validation before continuing trials on new version. (6) Archive: post-decision archive (EX-015) must include clause-lib version pin in metadata.

findings.finding_title: SwarmAwareSORA feature is over-specified (47 lines) while Phase 3 Archive feature is under-specified (1 feature); detail-level imbalance creates navigation friction
consistency_issues: RSPEC-010 (SwarmAwareSORA) contains 47 lines of Given/When/Then with 8+ sub-steps; most other features are 15–25 lines
Phase 3 archive folder contains only 1 feature file (post_decision_archive.feature) covering 2 entirely different concerns: data retention duration and post-incident AAIB extraction
Isabel-examples.json provides 15 worked examples, but distribution is uneven: 5 examples for completeness check (Phase 1), 3 for containment (Phase 2), 0 for Phase 3 archive
findings.reasoning: A developer navigating the feature files encounters SwarmAwareSORA with 47 lines and thinks 'this must be really complex', then navigates to Phase 3 and finds only 1 feature file for 2 different concerns. This creates a false sense of importance (SwarmAwareSORA is complex because over-specified, not because it's intrinsically harder than other features) and undersells Phase 3 (archive seems like an afterthought). The imbalance suggests incomplete specification in Phase 3.
findings.recommendation: Rebalance detail levels: (1) Break SwarmAwareSORA into 2 separate features: 'swarm-aware-hazard-analysis' (SAIL mapping) and 'swarm-specific-failure-modes' (loss-of-separation cascade). (2) Expand Phase 3 from 1 feature to 3: 'data-retention-and-security.feature', 'post-incident-aaib-extraction.feature', 'forensic-trail-reconstruction.feature'. (3) Add 3 worked examples for Phase 3 (currently 0 examples). (4) Limit feature files to 20–30 lines; break longer scenarios into separate features.

findings.finding_title: Glossary term hyphenation and acronym expansion inconsistent across feature files; pre-commit hook should enforce style
consistency_issues: Chris-language.json glossary defines 'meaningful-human-control' (hyphenated), but features sometimes use 'meaningful human control' (3 words) and sometimes 'meaningful-human-control'
'swarm-aware' is sometimes hyphenated, sometimes written as 'swarm aware' (2 words)
Acronyms: SORA is sometimes expanded on first use ('Specific Operational Risk Assessment – a framework...'), sometimes not
C2 link: sometimes 'C2 link', sometimes 'C2 Link' (capitalization inconsistent)
findings.reasoning: A CAA case officer reading two different feature files encounters 'meaningful human control' in one and 'meaningful-human-control' in another, and wonders if they are different concepts. This undermines confidence in the glossary and creates ambiguity about which definition applies. Consistency is not just style — it affects interpretability.
findings.recommendation: Implement pre-commit hook (Python script, ~6 development hours) that: (1) Validates every bolded term against glossary.md; (2) Enforces hyphenation/capitalization style from glossary; (3) Flags unexpanded acronyms on first use; (4) Rejects commits if inconsistencies found. Hook should provide diffs showing required fixes. Alternative: Run nightly linter on feature files and report style violations in daily standup.

findings.finding_title: PackCompleteness 'complete' term has dual meaning (application intake vs incident data recording); glossary distinguishes but feature specs do not
findings.feature: F02: Completeness Check (REQ001) and F13: Incident Data Recording (REQ012)
consistency_issues: Chris-language.json lists TWO definitions: 'Operational Authorisation pack' (complete = all mandatory documents + substantive content) and 'complete-recorded-evidence' (complete = comprehensive telemetry + video + audio + state logs)
DeFOSPAM Dorothy-8 flagged with confidence 10/10: 'Complete has two distinct meanings. REQ001 (application) context vs REQ012 (incident) context.'
rex-refined.json RSPEC-002 (completeness check) and RSPEC-045 (incident data) both use term 'complete' but with different implied meanings
Vibe 'PackCompleteness' scale addresses REQ001 (application intake) but there is no separate scale for 'complete-recorded-evidence' (incident data)
findings.reasoning: A case officer reading the completeness check feature for applications ('all 8 mandatory artefacts present') will have a different mental model than a flight-ops engineer implementing incident recording ('comprehensive telemetry with sampling rates defined'). These are NOT the same thing, but the glossary uses the same word for both. This creates potential for misunderstanding across teams.
findings.recommendation: Rename the incident recording term to 'comprehensive-forensic-evidence' or 'complete-flight-data' to avoid ambiguity. Update: (1) chris-language.json: add new term 'complete-flight-data' with distinct definition; mark old 'complete-recorded-evidence' as deprecated; (2) rex-refined.json: update RSPEC-045 to use 'complete-flight-data' instead of 'complete-recorded-evidence'; (3) feature files: ensure features/phase1_applicant/ uses 'Operational Authorisation pack' and features/phase3_post_decision/ uses 'complete-flight-data'. (4) Add note to glossary.md explaining the distinction.

findings.finding_title: Public engagement and classified MOD evidence handling conflict—unclear how classified trials can be publicly engaged
findings.feature: F18 (Public & Stakeholder Engagement), F24 (Classification-Aware Evidence Segregation)
findings.business_goal: GOAL-010 (Public Trust & Stakeholder Engagement) vs. GOAL-009 (Manage Classified Evidence)
findings.value_statement: GOAL-010 requires public engagement and CAP 1616 community consultation for all trials. GOAL-009 requires classification handling per CAP 722H for MOD-sensitive evidence. For Protector RG Mk1 trials (MOD program), evidence will include classified platform specs, sensor capabilities, and operational scenarios. How can public engagement proceed when trial evidence is classified? Scope conflict: Can classified trials be publicly engaged? If yes, what evidence can be disclosed? If no, how does CAA meet CAP 1616 public engagement obligation?
findings.reasoning: REQ017 (Public Engagement) and REQ018 (Security Classification) are both documented but scope boundaries not addressed. Vibe Requirements note: 'PublicEngagementCoverage and Classification-Aware Evidence Segregation have no stated conflict resolution or precedence rules.' DeFOSPAM missing-feature analysis: no feature addresses interaction between REQ017 and REQ018. Parent user-story context mentions 'Protector RG Mk1 transition' and 'MOD programme leadership' indicating MOD programs will be in scope. Regulatory precedent: UK Defence trials typically have limited public disclosure; however, CAP 1616 assumes civil operation transparency.
findings.recommendation: Before F18 and F24 can be implemented, CAA and MOD must jointly publish Engagement Policy for Classified Trials specifying: (1) Public engagement scope: what trial information can be disclosed (location, dates, aircraft count, but not sensor specs or platform capabilities)? (2) Community notification: can landowners be notified if classification restricts disclosure? (3) Press strategy: can media attend observation? (4) GDPR scope: is flight data classified? Can community feedback be disclosed? (5) Precedence: if classification conflicts with CAP 1616 engagement, what is default (classification takes precedence, or engagement requirement overrides classification limits)? Recommend CAA coordinate with MOD PR and DCSA (Defence Countermeasures and Support Activity) on media handling. Until policy is published, Feature Request: Workbench should include 'Classification-Engagement Policy Placeholder' that flags trials with both classified evidence and public engagement requirements, and prompts operator to confirm engagement plan with MOD liaison before CAA approval.

findings.finding_title: Operator rework acceptance mismatch—applicant expects 80% completeness with remediation acceptable; regulator expects 100% first-submission completeness
findings.value_statement: Vibe Requirements PackCompleteness stakeholder analysis identifies conflict: 'Applicant wants flexibility on first submission (incomplete pack with remediation path acceptable); Case Officer wants 100% completeness on submission (no rework cycles). Conflict resolved by Case Officer authority.' However, this conflict is unresolved in requirements; both expectations are documented without explicit resolution. This will create friction during Workbench deployment: operators will submit incomplete packs expecting revision requests and resubmission opportunity; case officers will reject incomplete packs expecting re-engagement and resubmission. Gap: who decides what is 'acceptable incomplete' for remediation? What triggers rejection vs. revision request?
findings.reasoning: Vibe Requirements PackCompleteness definition explicitly notes conflict: 'Applicant tolerable_override: Lower bar on first submission (80% completeness with remediation plan acceptable)' vs. constraint 'Tolerable = 100% per Alexa's scale.' Target is 90% first-submission completeness (wish) and 100% (goal), implying current state (72%) leaves 28% gap that will be filled by revision cycles. Performance Engineering analysis: no NFR specified for revision cycle time or cost. DeFOSPAM analysis: no feature explicitly handles revision request flow (F25 'Operator-Private Feedback' is separate from F02 Completeness Check).
findings.recommendation: Define explicit revision policy: (1) Completeness threshold: submit pack must contain >80% of mandatory documents (Quick Gate), with identified gaps flagged to operator within 24 hours. (2) Remediation window: operator has 14 days to resubmit missing documents or provide timeline for delivery (e.g., ConOps draft by 2026-05-30, final by 2026-06-15). (3) Revision request limit: operator may resubmit up to 3 times before case is rejected and must be resubmitted from scratch. (4) Escalation: if pack remains <80% after 30 days, CAA initiates resubmission or case closure. Vibe target revision: 90% first-submission completeness applies to packs that pass >80% Quick Gate, not raw intake submissions. This clarification should be reflected in F02 acceptance criteria and Meter definition.

findings.finding_title: Duty Holder accountability and signature requirements not explicit in REQ001 pack-completeness criteria; applicants unaware that ConOps, SORA, and Safety Case require executive sign-off
findings.reasoning: DeFOSPAM Dorothy-8 noted that 'complete' is undefined and acceptance criteria are absent from REQ001. Vibe PackCompleteness scale includes 'PackAcceptanceCriteriaMet' but does not specify criteria. Example EX-012 (Scotland resubmission failure) shows Duty Holder signature on Safety Case is mandatory, but this requirement is not stated in REQ001 or ConOps guidance. METER-002 (Paragraph-Level Coverage Audit) checks for substantive content but does not explicitly verify signatures. Applicants in EX-001 (Scotland) had 3 qualified pilots signing off, but future applicants may not realize signature is a gate. Example EX-013 shows Duty Holder signature required for conditional approval decision.
findings.recommendation: Update REQ001 user story to explicitly include: 'ConOps must be signed by trial pilot or chief pilot; SORA must be signed by SORA assessor; Safety Case must be signed by Safety Engineer or COO; all signatures must be dated and in original (scanned acceptable, but typed name without image is insufficient).' Publish CAP 722A Appendix D (Signature Requirements) specifying: (1) artefact-by-artefact signature requirements (which documents, which roles). (2) signature format (hand signature image, date, printed name and title). (3) verification process (CAA checks signature against corporate signing authority list on intake). (4) consequence of missing signature (mandatory gate, case cannot proceed to next phase until obtained). Update METER-002 checklist to include signature verification as sub-scale gate. Publish by 2026-Q2.

findings.finding_title: Applicant feedback segregation requires careful step design
findings.feature_file: Not present in current feature set (should be F25: Operator-Private Feedback vs Regulator Decision Record)
findings.reasoning: Grace's goals and existing features (F25, F13) mention separation of applicant feedback from binding regulatory decisions. Current feature set does not include scenarios for this segregation. Feature file gap: scenarios for feedback-to-decision boundary are missing. This is material to 'defensible approval' goal because audit trails must show feedback (non-binding) vs decisions (binding) clearly separated.
findings.recommendation: Create feature file 'features/panel_review/feedback_decision_segregation.feature' with scenarios: (1) Applicant submits clarification request (non-binding feedback). (2) Case officer updates feedback status. (3) Feedback does NOT appear in final decision record (only in feedback log). (4) Decision record references feedback but does not include feedback text (immutable separation). (5) Audit trail shows feedback timeline separately from decision timeline. Estimated effort: 6-8 hours for feature file + step definitions.

findings.finding_title: Classification-tier scenario coverage gap: must ensure 80%+ of classification scenarios test across all 3 tiers (OFFICIAL, OFFICIAL-SENSITIVE, SECRET)
findings.scenario: RSPEC-063 (Classification custodian: SECRET evidence triggers CAP 722H) + all classification-aware scenarios
risk_factors: security_classification_boundary (MOD trials with classified evidence require special handling; GOAL-009 requires 100% coverage)
tier_transition_logic (SECRET → redacted summary, OFFICIAL-SENSITIVE → filtered view, OFFICIAL → full access; tier logic must be tested across transitions)
findings.mitigation: Expand classification-aware scenarios in full suite: (1) OFFICIAL-only evidence (should have no CRO escalation), (2) OFFICIAL-SENSITIVE evidence (should trigger CRO, redacted summary generated), (3) SECRET evidence (should escalate to cleared subset only). Test evidence upload → classification detection → tier assignment → access control enforcement for each tier. Snapshot test classification outcome per document type (ConOps, Risk Register, etc.). Validate that tier-aware evidence segregation actually enforces access restrictions (uncleared user cannot access SECRET pool). Target: 80%+ of classification scenarios test all 3 tiers; validate tier transitions explicitly.
findings.reasoning: Current coverage: 2 classification scenarios (RSPEC-063 + one other). Target: expand to 5-8 scenarios covering tier transitions. For each tier, test: (1) evidence upload → detection, (2) classification outcome → access control, (3) redaction logic (if tier-specific), (4) audit trail (who accessed what, when). Validate that CRO role is enforced (uncleared users cannot view SECRET evidence).
findings.recommendation: Implementation: (1) Expand full suite with 5 new classification-tier scenarios: OFFICIAL-only (happy path), OFFICIAL-SENSITIVE-only (CRO escalation), SECRET (full security path), OFFICIAL ↔ OFFICIAL-SENSITIVE transition (evidence reclassification), cross-tier access attempt (uncleared user tries to access SECRET, should fail). (2) Mock CRO and Classification Review Officer role; test redaction rules per CAP 722H. (3) Snapshot test classification outcome per document type. (4) Validate access control: OFFICIAL accessible to all, OFFICIAL-SENSITIVE accessible only to cleared-CAA staff, SECRET accessible only to cleared-CAA + cleared-MAA staff. (5) Allure dashboard: add panel 'Classification Tier Coverage' showing % of scenarios testing each tier. (6) Target: >80% of classification scenarios test all 3 tiers.

findings.finding_title: Allure dashboard must display 8 custom panels to support specification health monitoring and override calibration feedback loop
findings.scenario: All test runs (smoke, regression, full) feeding into Allure Report
risk_factors: visibility_gap (current Allure default dashboard doesn't expose flake rate, override rate, clause-lib version, classification tier coverage)
feedback_loop_closure (GOAL-015 panel override audit requires visibility of override frequency and reasoning; dashboard must surface calibration signals)
findings.mitigation: Create 8 custom Allure dashboard panels: (1) pass_rate_by_feature (PASS% per feature F01-F30), (2) avg_exec_time_by_suite (p50, p95 latency), (3) flake_rate_30d_rolling (std dev of daily pass rate), (4) coverage_by_req_ac_keystone (% scenarios covering REQ001-REQ020, AC1-AC8, K1-K8), (5) clause_lib_version_pin (version string per run), (6) override_calibration_rate (panel override decisions / total decisions, audit trail %), (7) perf_gate_status (red/yellow/green per Gate 1-8), (8) classification_tier_coverage (% scenarios testing each tier). Each panel updates per test run (or daily for rolling metrics). Set alert thresholds: flake rate >2%, any perf gate red, clause-lib version mismatch, override rate >5%.
findings.reasoning: Specification health is only maintained if validation feedback is continuously visible. DeFOSPAM critical findings (swarm-aware undefined, meaningful human control undefined, containment undefined) suggest regulatory ambiguity will drive override decisions; dashboard visibility of override frequency helps calibrate requirements clarity. Isabel's examples (EX-011 panel override audit, EX-015 post-decision archive) emphasize governance accountability; dashboard surfaces override trends. Cerf's performance gates need visibility (all 8 gates must be green for release). Flake rate tracking (30-day rolling) identifies intermittent failures early. Coverage metrics (REQ/AC/keystone %) validate specification completeness.
findings.recommendation: Implementation: (1) Add 8 custom Allure dashboard plugins (or use Allure API to generate HTML panels). (2) Feed metrics from test runner: JUnit XML + custom JSON artifacts. (3) Update frequency: per-test-run for functional pass rate, daily aggregation for 30-day rolling flake rate. (4) Thresholds: flake rate >2% → email alert, perf gate red → Slack + PagerDuty, clause-lib version mismatch → email. (5) Panel design: each panel displays trend line (last 30 days) + heatmap (pass % per feature per day). (6) Override panel: display override decisions with timestamp, actor, justification text; trend line shows override rate (should be <5% healthy). (7) Performance gate panel: display p95 latency vs threshold for each gate; color code (green <threshold, yellow 80-100% threshold, red >threshold). (8) Release readiness checklist: all panels green, flake rate <2%, clause-lib version pinned, override rate <5%.

findings.finding_title: Bidirectional cross-references incomplete; geofence features do not reference termination features as fallback mechanism
findings.feature: F06: Geofence & Containment, F12: Flight Termination
consistency_issues: F06 geofence_containment.feature references F12 independent termination as 'fallback if geofence fails'
F12 flight_termination.feature does NOT reference F06 geofence as 'primary containment mechanism'
Navigation: Reader cannot discover relationship by reading F12 alone; must read F06 first
findings.reasoning: A developer building the termination system reads flight_termination.feature and understands that termination is the ultimate safety net, but doesn't discover that geofence is the primary containment mechanism (termination is only fallback). This creates incomplete mental model of the containment architecture. Bi-directionality is important for completeness and discoverability.
findings.recommendation: Add explicit cross-references to all feature files: at the top of flight_termination.feature, add 'Related features: F06 (geofence as primary containment). At the top of geofence_containment.feature, add 'Related features: F12 (termination as fallback). This can be automated in the Relish web interface (feature dependency graph) or manually maintained as hyperlinks in Markdown. Estimated effort: 4 hours to audit all features and add missing cross-references.

findings.finding_title: Phase 3 (Post-Decision) feature files lack 'Why' preamble linking to Goals; readability impact for new team members
findings.feature: F22: Post-Trial Lessons-Learned & Close-out Archive, F28: Case History & Revision Management, F30: Audit Logging & Forensic Trail
consistency_issues: Phase 1 and Phase 2 features start with narrative explaining business context (e.g., 'This feature supports GOAL-001: Build Defensible Approval Throughput')
Phase 3 features lack this narrative; readers cannot immediately connect feature to goal or understand regulatory importance
EX-015 (post-decision-archive example) is the only reference to the goal connection, but examples are separate from feature files
findings.reasoning: A new developer reading post_decision_archive.feature without the Goal context might think 'why is data retention so important?' or 'what happens if we skip archival?'. Without the 'Why' preamble, Phase 3 features feel like technical implementation detail rather than regulatory requirement. This hurts understanding of regulatory criticality and makes it harder to prioritize Phase 3 work.
findings.recommendation: Add Goal narrative preamble to all Phase 3 features following the template: '@phase3 ... Feature: Feature Name\n  This feature supports GOAL-005 (Enable Incident Investigation & Regulatory Closure)\n  by ensuring that [regulatory requirement]...'. Update affected features: (1) post_trial_archive.feature → add GOAL-005 context; (2) case_history_management.feature → add GOAL-012 context; (3) audit_logging.feature → add GOAL-012 context. Effort: 2 hours.

findings.finding_title: Phase 2 feature files lack mapping of deep-dive reviewers to assessment domains (SORA, safety, autonomy, ML); feature organization suggests unclear specialization
consistency_issues: Grace-goals.json lists 6 deep-dive review goals (GOAL-002 SORA, GOAL-003 Containment, GOAL-004 Autonomy, etc.) but feature file organization does not mirror these goals
Feature folder structure is flat (step6_deep_dive_reviews/) rather than hierarchical (step6_deep_dive_reviews/sora/, step6_deep_dive_reviews/containment/, step6_deep_dive_reviews/autonomy/)
No clear mapping of features to reviewer specialization (who is the SORA assessor? who is the autonomy engineer? who is the ML specialist?)
findings.reasoning: A CAA program manager trying to understand the deep-dive review step reads the flat folder structure and wonders: Are all these reviews happening in parallel or sequentially? Are they independent or interdependent? Who is responsible for each review? Feature file organization can either clarify or obscure process structure.
findings.recommendation: Reorganize features/phase2_regulator/step6_deep_dive_reviews/ into domain subfolders: (1) sora/ (SORA assessment features), (2) safety/ (safety case and containment reviews), (3) autonomy/ (swarm decision-making, emergent behavior), (4) human_factors/ (meaningful human control, workload), (5) ml/ (model manifest, explainability). Add README.md in step6_deep_dive_reviews/ listing the reviewer roles and dependencies. Effort: 3 hours (mostly folder reorganization and README authoring).
